gaps.description: Attack paths lack architectural specificity - generic paths don't reflect web application threat landscape
gaps.recommendation: Develop web-app specific attack paths (SQL injection, XSS, authentication bypass, file upload attacks)

gaps.description: 17 controls for 3-node architecture is over-engineered; missing critical web-specific protections
gaps.recommendation: Focus on 8-10 web-critical controls: WAF, input validation, SQL injection protection, session management, HTTPS enforcement

gaps.description: Validation shows INVALID status with 12 issues despite node count match
gaps.recommendation: Debug validation issues - check control placement, connection accuracy, and diagram syntax

gaps.description: Risk scores appear miscalibrated for minimal web architecture complexity
gaps.recommendation: Recalibrate RAPIDS scores based on actual attack surface - simple web app should have lower baseline risks

strengths: All 10 artifacts present and properly formatted
Comprehensive documentation with technical and executive perspectives
Phased action plan with realistic timelines and cost estimates
Strong risk reduction achieved (36→6.7 points, 81.4% improvement)

recommendations.action: Redesign attack paths for web application context
recommendations.verification: Verify attack paths include web-specific techniques: T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1059 (Command Injection), T1566 (Phishing), with realistic hop counts 2-4

recommendations.action: Right-size control recommendations for architecture complexity
recommendations.verification: Reduce to 8-10 controls focused on web security: WAF, input validation, authentication, encryption, logging, backup, patching, monitoring
Achieve validation status VALID with <5 issues and confidence >95%

recommendations.action: Recalibrate RAPIDS risk scores for architecture complexity
recommendations.verification: Adjust baseline risks: APPLICATION_VULNS 40-50, RANSOMWARE 30-40 for minimal web app

gaps.description: LEAST PRIVILEGE claims T1567 ← mitigated by M1021, but M1021 not in T1567's MITRE mitigation list (only M1021, M1057 valid)
gaps.recommendation: Remove M1021 from T1567 mapping or replace with M1057 (Data Loss Prevention)

gaps.description: VULNERABILITY SCANNING claims T1213 ← mitigated by M1017, but M1017 not in T1213's MITRE mitigation list (valid: M1032, M1060, M1017, M1054, M1018, M1047, M1041)
gaps.recommendation: Verify M1017 mapping - actually valid per MITRE reference, no action needed

gaps.description: T1005 and T1567 have minimal mitigation coverage (1 control each) despite appearing in high-risk attack paths
gaps.recommendation: Add data loss prevention controls (M1057) and access controls (M1032) for better coverage

gaps.description: Ground truth validation shows 12 issues with generic techniques, affecting overall validation confidence (99.5% but marked INVALID)
gaps.recommendation: Review attack path construction to use more specific sub-techniques where available

strengths: Defense-in-depth strategy with controls implementing multiple mitigations (e.g., LEAST PRIVILEGE: M1016, M1018, M1026, M1042)
Critical techniques well-covered: T1059 (6 controls), T1190 (5 controls), T1213 (4 controls)
Risk reduction calculation realistic at 81.4% given extensive control coverage
Proper control prioritization with critical controls addressing highest-impact techniques

recommendations.action: Fix T1567 mitigation mapping for LEAST PRIVILEGE control
recommendations.verification: Verify T1567 mitigations in MITRE ATT&CK reference (M1021, M1057 only)

recommendations.action: Add data loss prevention controls for T1005 and T1567 coverage
recommendations.verification: Implement M1057 (Data Loss Prevention) controls and verify coverage

recommendations.action: Refine attack paths to use specific sub-techniques instead of generic techniques
recommendations.verification: Replace generic techniques with specific sub-techniques and re-run validation

gaps.description: Generic MITRE technique mappings indicate potential control bypass opportunities for skilled attackers
Path #1 bypasses WAF and MFA protections, relying only on EDR and encryption

strengths: Multi-layered defense with WAF blocking web exploits
EDR providing endpoint detection and response
Encryption protecting data at rest and in transit
